title: Delay (Time Lag)

content.statement: Cause and effect are often separated in time.
content.question: What system are you about to quit too early because you don't trust the delay?
content.equation: Perceived Effect = Actual Effect ÷ Time Delay
content.mental_model: Plant today, harvest later, don't dig up the seed.
content.life_lens: Weight loss, skin, marriage, delayed feedback.
sop: Pick one system where results take time to show up, such as content, relationships, health, or referrals.
Estimate the realistic Time Delay for that system in weeks (see calculation, page 5).
Track your input consistency, whether you did the work, instead of watching for outcome movement.
Set a review date at the expected delay point, not before.
Don't quit a system before its expected delay has actually passed.
questions: What system are you about to quit too early because you don't trust the delay?
Which effort have you already abandoned that was probably about to pay off, if you'd just waited?
What's the realistic time lag between doing this consistently and actually seeing it show up?
content.equation_example: Actual Effect rated 8 out of 10, Time Delay 12 weeks. Perceived Effect = 8 divided by 12 = 0.67. A low number means the real effect is being diluted by a long delay, it'll feel like nothing's working even though it is. A high number, a short delay, means effects are felt almost immediately, which also means people underestimate how much of their success came from a system versus luck.
content.closing_statement: Judge systems by their trajectory, not this week's result.

caption:
Principle Nº 116: Delay (Time Lag)

Cause and effect are often separated in time.

Ask yourself: What system are you about to quit too early because you don't trust the delay?
